narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 3, which is below the typical 5.7 average, a notably low Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the usual 5.5, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 27 well under the 36.7 average, an undergraduate GPA of 2.9 versus the 3.2 norm, and plans to attend part-time rather than the full-time path chosen by over 92% of students. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 58% chance you'll fail the bar—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as schools prioritize those likely to pass and practice effectively. The decision hinged most on your Year 3 academic decile, which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure, pulling the prediction firmly in that direction despite your other qualifications. Your undergraduate GPA played the second-biggest role, but in a positive way, as it nudged the outlook toward passing and helped offset some concerns. Being in the part-time track came third in influence, contributing somewhat toward the failure prediction since it's uncommon and linked in the data to tougher outcomes. Compared to typical admitted students, your lower Year 3 standing and part-time status stand out as bigger drags than your solid middle-income background or LSAT, even though those trail the averages too. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male with clear drive—it's the model weighing patterns from thousands of similar paths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost toward the average, strengthening your recent grades through post-bac courses or targeted prep to lift that Year 3 decile equivalent, and exploring full-time options if feasible. Many in your position have succeeded by addressing these key areas, reapplying stronger next cycle. Your GPA shows you can compete academically, so building on that foundation could flip the model's view. Keep pushing; law schools value persistence from determined applicants like you.
